coredata.dc:title: Holey reduced graphene oxide inducing sensitivity enhanced detection nanoplatform for cadmium ions based on glutathione-gold nanocluster
coredata.dc:creator: Mo, Fengye
Ma, Zhangyan
Wu, Tengteng
Liu, Meiling
Zhang, Youyu
Li, Haitao
Yao, Shouzhuo
coredata.dc:description: Heavy metal contamination is a widely focused topic in environmental analysis and health care in recent years. Herein, a new fluorescence (FL) nanoplatform for sensitive detection of Cd2+ was developed based on AuNCs and holey reduced graphene oxide (HRGO). HRGO was obtained by hydrothermal method using graphene oxide (GO) as the precursor, H2O2 as an etchant and ascorbic acid (AA) as a reducing agent. GSH-AuNCs were synthesized by using glutathione (GSH) as the template and reducing agent. The FL of AuCNs were quenched by HRGO, which may result from fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between AuNCs and HRGO and then the FL was recovered when adding Cd2+ in the system owing to the formation of Cd-GSH complex. In the presence of the holey, inter-crossing and open structure of the HRGO, the HRGO-AuNCs FL-OFF-ON nanoplatform has larger FL quench efficiency than that of GO. At the same time, the nanoplatform can further provide a more convenient environment for Cd2+ detection with improved detection sensitivity compared with those in the presence of GO or direct detection of Cd2+ using AuNCs. At the optimized concentration of HRGO, the fluorescence intensity of HRGO-AuNCs increased with the concentration of Cd2+ and the linear range was 0.1-100 μM with the detection limit of 42.0 nM. Moreover, the nanoplatform can be used for Cd2+ in real samples with good accuracy and excellent recovery. Therefore, the attempt of HRGO in fluorescence nanoplatform may be expanded to other optical sensing areas or medicine delivery and has great prospective.
coredata.dcterms:subject: GSH-AuNCs
HRGO
Cd2+
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer
Fluorescence enhancement

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Cd2+ in real samples
Detection of Cd2+ in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21645008 , 21305042 , 21375037 ), the Scientific Research Fund of Hunan Provincial Education Department ( 14B116 ), the Science and Technology Department ( 14JJ4030 ), the Opening Fund of State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics, Hunan University ( 2013017 ), Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education of China) , Hunan Normal University ( KLCBTCMR2018-08 ) and key laboratory of Jiangxi Province Persistent Pollutants Control and Resources Recycle ( ST201422007 ), Collaborative Innovation Center of New Chemical Technologies for Environmental Benignity and Efficient Resource Utilization and the Aid Program for Science and Technology Innovative Research Team in Higher Educational Institutions of Hunan Province .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–vis (a), excitation (b) and emission (c) spectra of GSH-AuNCs. (Inset: the photograms of GSH-AuNCs under daylight and UV light. (B) FTIR spectra of GSH (a) and GSH-AuNCs (b). (C) The TEM image of GSH-AuNCs. (D) XPS spectrum of Au 4f.
(A) The UV–vis spectra of GO (a) and HRGO (b). Insets are the photogram of GO (c) and HRGO (d). (B) FTIR spectra of GO (a) and HRGO (b). (C) The TEM images of GO and (D) HRGO.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of GSH-AuNCs (a), GO-AuNCs (b), HRGO-AuNCs (c); (B) Fluorescence quenching efficiency of GO (a) and HRGO (b) at the given concentrations. (C) fluorescence lifetime of AuNCs and HRGO-AuNCs.
Fluorescence (A) and UV–vis spectra (B) of AuNCs, HRGO, Cd2+, HRGO-AuNCs, and HRGO-AuNCs-Cd2+. The concentration of Cd2+ is 50 μM.
(A) Changes in fluorescence intensity with different concentrations of Cd2+. (B) The linear relationship between (F-F 0 )/F 0 and concentration of Cd2+.
The synthesis of HRGO (A) and the schematic illustration of fluorescence detection of Cd2+ on the basis of GSH-AuNCs and HRGO (B).
The determination of Cd2+ in real samples using the as-proposed GSH-AuNCs- HRGO and the ICP-MS for comparison.

head.ce:title: Holey reduced graphene oxide inducing sensitivity enhanced detection nanoplatform for cadmium ions based on glutathione-gold nanocluster

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology & Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education China, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University, Changsha 410081, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology & Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry ofEducation China
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contribute equal to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Heavy metal contamination is a widely focused topic in environmental analysis and health care in recent years. Herein, a new fluorescence (FL) nanoplatform for sensitive detection of Cd2+ was developed based on AuNCs and holey reduced graphene oxide (HRGO). HRGO was obtained by hydrothermal method using graphene oxide (GO) as the precursor, H2O2 as an etchant and ascorbic acid (AA) as a reducing agent. GSH-AuNCs were synthesized by using glutathione (GSH) as the template and reducing agent. The FL of AuCNs were quenched by HRGO, which may result from fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between AuNCs and HRGO and then the FL was recovered when adding Cd2+ in the system owing to the formation of Cd-GSH complex. In the presence of the holey, inter-crossing and open structure of the HRGO, the HRGO-AuNCs FL-OFF-ON nanoplatform has larger FL quench efficiency than that of GO. At the same time, the nanoplatform can further provide a more convenient environment for Cd2+ detection with improved detection sensitivity compared with those in the presence of GO or direct detection of Cd2+ using AuNCs. At the optimized concentration of HRGO, the fluorescence intensity of HRGO-AuNCs increased with the concentration of Cd2+ and the linear range was 0.1-100 μM with the detection limit of 42.0 nM. Moreover, the nanoplatform can be used for Cd2+ in real samples with good accuracy and excellent recovery. Therefore, the attempt of HRGO in fluorescence nanoplatform may be expanded to other optical sensing areas or medicine delivery and has great prospective.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal ions such as Cd2+, Pb2+ and some organic pollutants in environment are significant risk to the health of human beings. For example, cadmium ions would be accumulated in the form of cadmium sulfur protein in liver, kidney, heart and other tissues or organs [1]. Moreover, the heart is very sensitive to cadmium ions, and even low dose of cadmium ions have toxic effects on organisms [2]. Therefore, analysis of Cd2+ would be an important topic of chemical and biological sensing. To date, electrochemical analysis methods and fluorescence methods are considered as commonly effective methods for Cd2+ detection. Compared with electrochemical methods, fluorescence methods can avoid the complex electrode preparation process and gain good sensitivity.

Up to now, fluorescent nanoplatforms were widely used in photodynamic therapy (PDT) and photothermal therapy (PTT) [3], homogeneous immunoassays [4], delivery of drugs, imaging in vivo [5] and sensing [6]. Such as platforms for luminescence resonance energy transfer (LRET)-based biosensing and bio-imaging [7], fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) platform for antigens detection [4] and pH tunable ﬂuorescent nanoplatforms for fundamental cell physiological processes [8]. Fluorescence-based detection strategies mainly include fluorescence enhancement [9], fluorescence quenching [10], ratio fluorescence [11], continuous detection [12] and so on. It is found that glycoproteins could enhance the fluorescence of mercaptophenyl boronic acid-capped Mn-doped ZnS quantum dots (QDs@MBA) through boronate-affinity [13]. Song et al. have synthesized green luminescent copolymer brushes of containing tris (8-hydroxyquinoline) aluminum on the graphene oxide (GO) surface and 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP) could quench the fluorescence via charge transfer to achieve its detection [14]. The fluorescence of dextran-fluorescein could be quenched on graphene surface and when organic pollutants were added, fluorescent probe detaches itself from graphene surface due to competitive interaction which can be used to qualify the organic pollutants [15]. To this end, it can be seen that organic dyes [16] quantum dots [17], nanoclusters [18], organic polymer [19] etc. are frequently used as fluorescent materials. Among them, metal nanoclusters have good water solubility and large Stokes shift, low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility, which can be used to detect metal cations such as Hg2+, Cu2+, anions such as I−, CN− and biological small molecules such as dopamine, melamine, and proteins [20].

Cations [21], anions [22], inorganic molecules [23], organic molecules [24], nanoparticles [25], nanosheets [26] etc. are often act as fluorescent quenchers. The quenching mechanisms include fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), inner filter effect (IFE), static quenching effect (SQE) and dynamic quenching effect (DQE). Typical two-dimensional materials such as graphene [14], manganese dioxide nanosheet [27], cobalt oxyhydroxide nanosheets [28], and molybdenum sulfide nanosheets [26] are widely fluorescent quenchers in construction of sensing platforms. Xiao et al. found that MnO2 nanosheets can quench the fluorescence of polydopamine nanoparticles through FRET effect [29]. Adenosine triphosphate (ATP) aptamers of double-stranded DNA could be absorbed onto the surface of GO through π-π stacking interaction, leading to fluorescence quenching of dsDNA-CuNCs. And when ATP was added, the specific interaction between ATP and its aptamer resulted in the recovery of fluorescence, which was expected to detect the protein kinases activity [30]. Therefore, the fluorescence platform constructed with the fluorescence materials and two-dimensional nanomaterials can provide a universal strategy for small molecules or biomolecules detection. Graphene is a single-atom-thick and two-dimensional carbon material that has attracted great attention because of its remarkable electronic, mechanical, and thermal properties [31]. It has been reported that graphene [32], GO [33] and rGO [34] could all act as fluorescence quencher. However, GO has limited quenching capability owing to the two-dimensional nanosheets inevitably reduce the surface area due to the restacking. On the contrary, the two-dimensional holey nanosheets can avoid this phenomenon and provide a more cross-over and open structure that exposes more active sites, provides lager surface and shorter lateral diffusion lengths for better charge transfer [35]. However, up to now, there was no relevant research involving the application of holey reduced graphene oxide (HRGO) in optical sensing, and most of the research applications were simply focused on electrochemical catalyst [36] or supercapacitors [37]. To this end, the development of the sensing platform based on the HRGO and expanding its application in analysis is of great importance.

Herein, a new fluorescence (FL) nanoplatform for sensitively detection of Cd2+ was developed based on AuNCs and HRGO. HRGO was obtained by hydrothermal method using graphene oxide (GO) as the precursor, H2O2 as an etchant and ascorbic acid (AA) as a reducing agent (Scheme 1 A). The as prepared HRGO was used as FL quencher for glutathione (GSH) functionalized-AuNCs, and the quench mechanism may ascribe as the FRET between GSH-AuNCs and HRGO. Then the FL was recovered when adding Cd2+ in the system owing to the in-situ formation of Cd-GSH complex at the surface of AuNCs to afford a core-shell heteronanostructure and eventually hampered the FRET between AuNCs and HRGO (Scheme 1B). Compared with GO, HRGO provides larger specific surface area and holes, which promises an enhanced fluorescence quenching efficiency. In addition, improved sensitivity and good selectivity was found when GSH-AuNCs-HRGO was used for Cd2+ detection, owing to the structure of the nanoplatform and recognition property of GSH to Cd2+. Therefore, the special HRGO could be prospect in sensing or biomedical areas.

Graphite was purchased from XFNANO Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China). Glutathione (GSH) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, USA). Chloroauric acid (HAuCl4), CdCl2·2.5H2O, ascorbic acid (AA) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and other reagents were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All reagents were analytical grades without purification. The Britton-Robison (BR) buffer solution was prepared by mixing the appropriate quantity of 0.2 M of acetic acid (HAc), boric acid (H3BO3), phosphate (H3PO4) and 0.04 M sodium hydroxide (NaOH). All solutions used in the experiments were prepared by Milli-Q ultrapure water (>18.2 MΩ).

Fluorescence spectrometer F-7000 (Hitachi, Japan) was applied for FL analysis. UV–vis spectra were obtained from UV-2450 (Shimadzu, Japan). Fourier infrared spectra were obtained using Nexus-870 (Thermo Nicolet, USA). Transmission electron microscope JEM-2100 (JEOL, Japan) were used for the characterization of the morphology of the material. ICP-MS (X series II/Thermo Fisher, USA) was applied for further confirmation of the concentration of Cd2+ in the real samples.

GSH-AuNCs were prepared according to the literature with slight modification [38]. All glassware used in the experiment were first soaked in aqua regia, followed by ultrasonic cleaning with ethanol and ultrapure water. GSH (0.3 mL, 100 mM), HAuCl4 (0.68 mL, 1%) and 9.02 mL of ultrapure water were mixed and vigorously stirred at 25 °C for 5 min, then heated to 70 °C and gently stirred for 24 h until the solution became pale yellow. The as obtained Au NCs were further purified by dialysis using 3.5 kDa dialysis membrane for 24 h and stored at 4 °C for further use.

Graphene oxide (GO) was prepared according to Hummers’ method with slight modifications [39]. GO solution (20 mL, 1 mg/L) was first mixed with H2O2 (2 mL, 0.3%) and etched at 180 °C for 6 h, then AA solution (2 mL, 0.1 M) was added into the mixture and reacted at 160 °C for 2 h. The final product HRGO was obtained by centrifuging and washing three times and dispersed in water.

HRGO solution with various concentration and GSH-AuNCs (70 μL) were added into 400 μL of BR buffer solution (pH 9.0), and the final volume was adjusted to 700 μL with ultrapure water. Then the mixture was incubated for 2 min at 25 °C and the fluorescence emission spectra were measured at 575 nm with the excitation wavelength of 397 nm.

HRGO (150 μL 0.2 mg/mL), GSH-AuNCs (70 μL) were mixed with 400 μL of BR buffer solution (pH 9.0) and incubated for 2 min at 25 °C. Then CdCl2 solutions with different concentration were added, and the total volume maintained at 700 μL and incubated at 25 °C for 9 min. The fluorescence spectra were recorded from 450 nm to 750 nm with the excite wavelength of 397 nm. The excitation and emission slits were set at 5 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cd2+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practical application of HRGO-AuNCs nanoplatform was determined by the determination of Cd2+ in tap, Taozi Lake and Xiangjiang River water samples. The real samples were firstly centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min, filtered with 0.25 μm membrane and diluted 100-fold with ultra-pure water, then 70 μL of water samples were mixed with GSH-AuNCs, HRGO and BR buffer solutions, which were similar to detection of Cd2+. Different concentrations of Cd2+ were added using the standard addition method and incubated at 25 °C for 9 min. Finally, fluorescence emission spectra were recorded on F-7000.

In the synthesis of GSH-Au NCs, GSH acts as template and reducing agent, which can reduce Au (III) and then aggregate to form ultra-small AuNCs. In addition, the -SH group in GSH can combine with Au to form strong S-Au bond and stabilize AuNCs. A sharp peak at ∼220 nm and a slight shoulder at 400 nm were found in the UV–vis spectra (Fig.1 A). The fluorescence excitation and emission wavelength were at 397 nm and 575 nm, respectively (Fig. 1A). Moreover, the insets are the photograms of GSH-AuNCs under daylight and UV light, which exhibited light yellow and emitted orange fluorescence under UV light (365 nm). Fig. 1B shows the FTIR spectra of GSH and GSH-AuNCs, and it is obvious that the characteristic absorption of -SH at 2527 cm−1 is found in GSH, which is disappeared in GSH-AuNCs, indicating that Au reacted with GSH through Au-S bonding [40]. It confirms that GSH plays an important role as the stabilizing agent and reductant. In addition, the TEM of GSH-AuNCs (Fig. 1C) shows that GSH-AuNCs are uniform in small size and exhibit good dispersion. XPS analysis was carried out to determine the oxidation state of Au atoms in the GSH-AuNCs. As shown in Fig. 1D, two intense peaks located at 83.28 and 86.88 eV are observed, which can be attributed to Au (0) and Au (I).

In this paper, HRGO was prepared by using GO as precursor, H2O2 as the etching agent and AA as the reducing agent. Fig. 2 A shows the UV–vis spectra of GO and HRGO. The UV–vis spectra of GO showed an obvious absorption peak at 232 nm, which was caused by π→π* transition of the CC bond, the small absorption peak around 300 nm by the n→π* transition of CO was also be found. For HRGO, since GO was reduced, the conjugation between the CC groups caused the red-shift to 256 nm. And the disappearance of the absorption peak around 300 nm also proved the reduction of CO bond. Fig. 2B shows the FTIR spectra of GO and HRGO, it was obviously that oxygen-containing functional groups of GO reduced. In addition, Fig. 2C and Fig. 2D are the TEM images of GO and HRGO, respectively. Fig. 2D shows holes on the surface of the graphene layers, which was obviously different from that of GO, indicating that graphene oxide nanosheets had been etched into holey graphene nanosheets.

Previous reports have shown that GO or graphene can quench the fluorescence of nanoclusters. In this work, we found that HRGO could also quench the fluorescence of GSH-AuNCs with higher efficiency. Fig. 3 A exhibits that the fluorescence of GSH-AuNCs in the presence of HRGO and GO at the same concentration of 34.3 μg/mL, which indicates the higher efficiency is obtained with HRGO. Fig. 3B further shows the comparison of the fluorescence quenching efficiency of HRGO and GO at different concentrations (8.6 μg/mL, 17.1 μg/mL, 25.7 μg/mL, 34.3 μg/mL, 42.9 μg/mL). It is obvious that the fluorescence quenching efficiency of HRGO was higher than that of GO at the same concentration in the evaluated range.

Up to now, the quenching mechanisms of graphene and graphene-like two-dimensional materials such as GO, graphene quantum dot (GQD) and graphitic-carbon nitride nanosheets (g-C3N4) were mainly based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [41]. Moreover, HRGO was synthesized using GO as the precursor and had larger specific surface area and holey structure, and therefore, we speculate that the quenching mechanism might also be FRET. This can be confirmed by fluorescence lifetime experiment (Fig. 3C). It demonstrated that the fluorescence lifetime decreased after adding HRGO to GSH-AuNCs to form HRGO-AuNCs-GSH. These results show that the addition of HRGO shortens the excited-state lifetime of the fluorescence GSH-AuNCs, indicating the occurrence of energy transfer between HRGO to GSH-AuNCs. Therefore, the fluorescence variation of GSH-AuNCs is result from the FRET between HRGO and GSH-AuNC.

As shown in Fig. 4 A, AuNCs exhibited significant fluorescence at 575 nm, while HRGO and Cd2+ show no fluorescence peak. When HRGO was added into GSH-AuNCs, the fluorescence of AuNCs was quenched. While adding Cd2+, the fluorescence intensity was enhanced with the enhance efficiency of 160%. Studies have shown that GSH and Cd2+ could form complexes with different structures under different pH conditions, which can cause different degrees of fluorescence enhancement [42]. It has been reported that Cd-GSH complex could form on the surface of GSH-AuNCs owing to the Cd2+ interact with sulfhydryl group of GSH in alkaline condition [43,44]. Therefore, we speculated the mechanism of the fluorescence enhancement of the HRGO-AuNCs system in the presence of Cd2+ was ascribed to the formation of the complex between Cd2+ and GSH, and the core-shell heteronanostructure of AuNCs and Cd-GSH can hamper the FRET between the HRGO and AuNCs owing to the increased distance. Therefore, the fluorescence of the GSH-AuNCs recovered.

In order to further explore the feasibility for detection of Cd2+, the control experiments were also conducted. As shown in Fig. S1, the systems of AuNCs and AuNCs-GO also showed sensitive response to Cd2+. For example, the fluorescence AuNCs and the AuNCs-GO can be enhanced when adding Cd2+. Compared with the system of HRGO-AuNCs, the fluorescence intensity of AuNCs and GO-AuNCs only enhanced 24.7% and 10.4% with the addition of Cd2+. These results suggested that the HRGO-AuNCs platform is the most sensitive. Fig. 4B shows the UV–vis spectra of AuNCs, HRGO, Cd2+, HRGO-AuNCs and HRGO-AuNCs-Cd2+. It was obviously that HRGO-AuNCs also had obvious absorption peaks at 220 nm and 257 nm, which were ascribed to GSH-AuNCs and HRGO. Moreover, the corresponding absorption peaks still existed after the addition of Cd2+, which indicated that no new substances were formed in the system after adding HRGO and Cd2+. HRGO could quench the fluorescence of GSH-AuNCs and Cd2+ could enhance the fluorescence of HRGO-AuNCs, and to this end, on the basis of HRGO-AuNCs-GSH, sensitive detection of Cd2+ may be realized.

To obtain good performance of the sensing system, several experimental conditions including temperature, pH, concentration of HRGO, fluorescence quenching time and recovery time were optimized. All the optimized conditions were based on fluorescence change efficiency ((F-F0)/F0 ). Fig. S2 A shows the relationship between the quenching efficiency and time, it was obvious that the fluorescence quenching was quite rapid and quickly. Hence, we chose 2 min as the reaction time for the fluorescence quenching procedure. Fig. S2B shows the relationship between the fluorescence enhancement efficiency and pH. It could be clearly seen that pH = 9.0 was the optimal pH. In addition, the temperature effect was studied, and the results are shown in Fig. S2C, which indicated that the temperature of 25 °C was the best condition. Fig. S2D shows the relationship between recovery time and the fluorescence enhancement efficiency. The FL intensity increased with time quickly before 9 min and gradually decreased after 9 min. The phenomenon might due to the aggregation effect, which lead to the weakening of the fluorescence intensity with longer time [45]. Therefore, the reaction time for Cd2+ and HRGO-GSH-AuNCs was 9 min.

As for the concentration of HRGO, Fig. S3 A shows the fluorescence quenching of AuNCs after adding different concentrations of HRGO at the above optimized condition. It was obvious that the quenching efficiency of GSH-AuNCs increased with the concentration of HRGO and the maximum quenching efficiency reached 90% (Fig. S3B) when the concentration of the HRGO was 57.1 μg/mL. Because the high quenching efficiency may influence the fluorescence recovery in the next step, and therefore, 42.9 μg/mL was selected as the concentration for the construction of HRGO-AuNCs platform to detect Cd2+ for the further experiment.

The anti-interference ability is the key for actual sample detection. In order to confirm the selectivity of HRGO-AuNCs for Cd2+ detection, some possible interfering metal ions including Ag+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Al3+, Hg2+,Cu2+ etc. with the concentration of 100 μM were chosen for study under the same conditions. Fig. S4 shows that no obvious changes of fluorescence intensity on HRGO-AuNCs in the presence of most of the cations, while Cd2+ with the concentration of 10 μM can significantly enhance the fluorescence of HRGO-AuNCs. It is reported that the complex of Cd-GSH can form on the surface of GSH-AuNCs owing to the specific interaction of Cd2+ to sulfhydryl group of GSH [43]. Hence, HRGO-AuNCs had a good selectivity towards Cd2+ over other metal cations, which was expected to be used in real samples. At the same time, under this condition, Cd(OH)2 can not be formed mainly because Cd2+ would preferentially coordinate with GSH rather than

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cd2+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The platform of HRGO-AuNCs in this study exhibited good selectivity toward Cd2+ and in order to evaluate the practical performance, real samples from tap water, lake and river water were detected (Table 1 ) via standard addition method. The recoveries are observed in the range of 95.6% to 100.8%, further indicating the reliability of this developed sensing system. Moreover, ICP-MS have been conducted to verify the analysis performance of our method and the results were displayed in Table 1, which suggested that our method is in accordance with ICP-MS. The detection limit is much lower than the standards for drinking water quality of the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) and China (GB 5749-2006), which suggest the content of Cd2+ should lower than 43.9 nM [33]. Therefore, it indicated that our method is sensitive enough for the purpose of detection of Cd2+ for the drinking water with good accuracy.

In this paper, a sensitive and selective nanoplatform of HRGO-AuNCs was proposed for determination of Cd2+ with excellent selectivity. The linear range of detection Cd2+ was 0.1 μM–100 μM with the detection limit of 42.0 nM, which was superior than many other sensors for Cd2+. Moreover, the real sample determination shows good recoveries. It is proved that HRGO is an excellent quencher for GSH-AuNCs and enhance the quench efficiency compared with GO, which can further improve the detection sensitivity for Cd2+. The holey structure and large specific surface area of HRGO was firstly applied in fluorescence sensing, which could broaden the application of HRGO beyond the electrochemical areas. It may also show its potential in other fluorescence nanoplatform and biosensing or biomedicine fields. Therefore, it is supposed that further study in medicine uploading and drug delivery based on HRGO are promising orientations that need to be explored.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21645008, 21305042, 21375037), the Scientific Research Fund of Hunan Provincial Education Department (14B116), the Science and Technology Department (14JJ4030), the Opening Fund of State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics, Hunan University (2013017), Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education of China), Hunan Normal University (KLCBTCMR2018-08) and key laboratory of Jiangxi Province Persistent Pollutants Control and Resources Recycle (ST201422007), Collaborative Innovation Center of New Chemical Technologies for Environmental Benignity and Efficient Resource Utilization and the Aid Program for Science and Technology Innovative Research Team in Higher Educational Institutions of Hunan Province.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.10.133.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
